commander_summary: Atlas has entered ELEVATED compound stress for the first time: two of three matrix legs breached, outflows $250M from CRITICAL, no active counter-pressure from any system operator. REDUCE_EXPOSURE. The core paradox is that the swap mechanism is processing redemptions without failure — validating the architecture's design — while the system-wide stress testing that mechanism is one moderate shock from exceeding any tested capacity. Kill Switches #2 (Yield Sustainability) and #3 (Reserve Coverage) are at WARNING, with collateral quality carrying a suspicious lack of quantified loss data. The single biggest unknown is the Foundation's capital runway to sustain the ~$2.87B annual yield subsidy — without this number, we cannot determine whether this stress resolves in days or escalates in weeks.

Bear pressure at 76 with MEDIUM confidence reflects multiple verified deteriorations across kill switches (two at WARNING) with significant unresolved uncertainties (Foundation runway, collateral quality, Consortium mandate). REDUCE_EXPOSURE is warranted because the risk/reward has shifted: the system could stabilize, but the probability-weighted downside (40% falsification) exceeds the upside (15% confirmation) by a margin that demands defensive positioning. INCREASE_MONITORING alone is insufficient given proximity to CRITICAL.

REDUCE_EXPOSURE. No reduction in yield. No visible deployment of reserves or emergency measures. $650M in redemptions processed through the swap mechanism without incident.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: The Foundation is maintaining the appearance of normalcy (unchanged yield, operational swap mechanism) while NOT taking defensive actions one would expect under stress (no new collateral acquisition, no reserve replenishment, no yield adjustment). Per LZ-EH-003, the inaction carries more evidentiary weight than the implicit stability message.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: Most rational explanation given observed behavior: the Foundation is pursuing a 'don't blink first' strategy — maintaining posted yield to prevent panic-driven outflows while hoping that the current outflow wave is temporary and self-limiting. Reducing the yield would be an explicit admission of stress, potentially triggering accelerated withdrawals. This is a classic game theory position where the cost of signaling weakness exceeds the cost of continuing the subsidy in the short term. However, this strategy has a finite runway dependent on Foundation capital (which is unknown). The strategy is rational IF the Foundation believes current outflows will stabilize AND it has sufficient capital to sustain the subsidy through the stress period. Both of these are unverified assumptions.

However, waiting too long risks the collateral losing additional value through mark-to-market deterioration.

At current deviation ($0.58), intervention would burn reserves for a relatively small peg deviation. (B) INABILITY — The Consortium may lack the operational capacity, mandate clarity, or capital to intervene effectively. Mark-to-market losses on existing collateral may have reduced their actual deployable capital below the reported $4.16B. I CANNOT resolve this without knowing: deployment mandate/triggers, actual realizable collateral value, and decision-making authority structure. The contradiction IS the finding.

The disconnect is stark: if operators genuinely believed outflows were temporary, there would be less urgency. But if they believed outflows were structural, reducing the yield would accelerate departures. They are in a classic trapped position. Per CL-002, I note the yield is unchanged and treat that as the only observable signal — I do not infer internal planning.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: The Facility Operators face a game-theoretic trap: (1) Maintaining 24.2% yield burns the subsidy at ~$2.87B/year but prevents panic-driven mass withdrawal. (2) Reducing yield would save subsidy but signal distress, potentially triggering the very outflow cascade they want to avoid. (3) The declining deposit base ($17.1B, down from higher levels) actually reduces the absolute subsidy cost — this is a partial natural brake. The most rational strategy IS to maintain yield while deposits decline, because each dollar of outflow reduces the subsidy burden. The Facility's problem resolves itself IF outflows stabilize at a lower deposit base — the same yield becomes cheaper to fund. The risk is that outflows don't stabilize and instead accelerate past the $2B critical threshold.

feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: The loop transitions to active_accelerating when: (a) Facility outflows breach $2B (only $250M away), causing redemption volumes to exceed swap mechanism tested capacity, OR (b) ASE price declines past 15% threshold, worsening the swap ratio to the point where massive new ASE minting is required per redemption, creating mechanical selling pressure that further depresses ASE. The current $650M in redemptions produced 6.5M new ASE (1.4% supply growth). At 5x this volume ($3.25B), supply growth would approach 7% — breaching the elevated threshold. The nonlinear aspect: as ASE price falls, MORE ASE must be minted per redemption, which creates MORE selling pressure on ASE, which requires MORE minting. This is a textbook reflexive feedback loop that is currently dormant-to-mild but has the architecture for self-reinforcement.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This loop is the primary mechanism by which orderly stress becomes disorderly. If the AFN peg breaks (thesis falsification), this loop is the most likely transmission mechanism. The swap mechanism's ability to absorb volume without triggering this reflexive spiral IS the core thesis claim. Currently being tested at moderate scale.

feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Transitions to active_accelerating if: (a) Foundation capital is revealed to be insufficient (we currently don't know), OR (b) yield is cut, triggering accelerated outflows that exceed the subsidy savings. The paradoxical brake: declining deposits reduce the absolute subsidy cost ($2.87B at current levels, less if deposits fall further). This could create a new equilibrium at lower deposit levels if the Foundation can sustain the lower absolute cost. The trigger to disorderly: if outflows accelerate faster than subsidy savings accrue, the Foundation faces an accelerating deficit even as deposits shrink.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The yield subsidy is the primary mechanism maintaining institutional deposits. Its depletion — whether sudden (cut) or gradual (Foundation capital exhaustion) — would test whether AFN has organic demand beyond yield arbitrage. Kill Switch #6 (Organic Demand) is UNKNOWN, which means the demand floor after subsidy removal is unknowable.

feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Transitions to active_accelerating if: (a) reserve coverage breaches 15% TRIGGERED threshold (currently 17.8%, only 2.8pp away), OR (b) collateral mark-to-market losses become public and quantified, revealing effective coverage is lower than reported 17.8%. The mechanism: lower reserves → lower confidence → more outflows → lower coverage ratio → even lower confidence. The Consortium's decision not to deploy means reserves are intact but unused — which preserves the ratio from declining further through deployment but also means there is no active counter-pressure against the confidence erosion.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: Reserve adequacy is the credibility backstop for the entire architecture. If reserves are perceived as insufficient, the peg has no fundamental support beyond the swap mechanism — which itself depends on ASE maintaining sufficient value. The reserve loop connects directly to the Outflow-Redemption-ASE loop through confidence transmission.

feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: At ~298 estimated depositors averaging ~$57M, a coordinated or coincidental withdrawal by 5-10 large depositors could breach the $2B critical outflow threshold (only $250M beyond current $1.75B). This loop is binary rather than gradual — it either happens or doesn't, driven by individual depositor decisions that are unobservable until executed. The trigger: any single depositor with >$250M deciding to withdraw would, by itself, push outflows past the critical threshold.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The concentrated depositor base creates tail risk that is structurally unmanageable. The system cannot absorb the simultaneous exit of even a small fraction of its depositor base without triggering compound stress chain escalation. This is a fundamental architectural fragility, not a cyclical risk.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Compound Stress Matrix Already at ELEVATED
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The simultaneous elevation of two legs is coincidental — the AFN deviation and Facility outflows are driven by different, unrelated causes and do not represent systemic stress.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: The two elevated legs are mechanically linked, not coincidental. Facility outflows ($1.75B) drive redemptions ($650M through swap), which create ASE selling pressure (11.8% decline), which worsens the swap ratio, which makes AFN holders less confident in the redemption mechanism, which widens the par deviation ($0.58). This is not coincidence — it is the system's feedback architecture operating as described in the thesis. The compound stress is structural, not random.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: An exogenous macro shock (not captured in current data) is simultaneously affecting all Atlas instruments, and the correlation is driven by the external factor rather than internal system dynamics.
assumptions: The observed price movements in AFN, ASE, and Facility flows are primarily driven by internal system dynamics rather than exogenous macro factors
evidence_citations: $1.75B Facility net outflows (direct measurement)
$650M AFN redemptions through swap mechanism producing 6.5M new ASE (direct measurement)
ASE 11.8% price decline concurrent with 1.4% supply growth from swap-minted ASE (direct measurement)
AFN par deviation at $0.58, widened from historical <$0.30 (direct measurement)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If the compound stress is structural and self-reinforcing, we should observe: (1) continued or accelerated outflows next period, (2) ASE supply growth rate increasing, (3) AFN deviation stable or widening. Conversely, if outflows pause, AFN deviation should narrow and ASE should stabilize — that would suggest the current stress was a discrete event rather than a cascade.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The mechanical linkage between the four observable data points (outflows → redemptions → ASE minting → ASE price decline → par deviation) follows the exact transmission mechanism described in the thesis. The null hypothesis of coincidence is implausible given the causal architecture connecting these instruments. One assumption required: that internal dynamics dominate over external macro factors. This is reasonable given the highly specific and interconnected nature of the movements, but I note it explicitly because exogenous macro data is absent from the dataset.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Facility Net Outflows Approaching Critical Range
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The $1.75B outflow represents normal institutional portfolio rebalancing — a few large depositors rotating out as part of routine allocation changes, not a systemic confidence signal.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Institutional depositors are making a rational risk-adjusted calculation: at 24.2% yield with a deteriorating peg ($0.58 deviation), declining reserves (17.8%), and no visible stabilization effort from the Consortium, the risk premium required to hold AFN Facility deposits has increased past the point where 24.2% compensates. The outflow is a repricing of risk, not routine rebalancing. Evidence: (1) This is the largest single-period outflow observed — 3.5x the elevated threshold. (2) The outflow is proportional across Facility and non-Facility (concentration stable at 73.1%), indicating system-wide reassessment. (3) The outflow occurs simultaneously with par deviation widening and ASE decline — consistent with a correlated confidence shock, not idiosyncratic rebalancing.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: A small number of very large depositors (5-10) withdrew for idiosyncratic reasons (fund mandate limits, liquidity needs), and the systemic interpretation is overstated.
assumptions: Depositors are withdrawing primarily due to Atlas-specific risk reassessment rather than idiosyncratic fund-level reasons
evidence_citations: $1.75B net outflows — largest single-period observed (direct measurement)
Stable 73.1% concentration ratio during outflows indicates proportional system-wide withdrawal (direct measurement)
Simultaneous AFN deviation widening and ASE decline concurrent with outflows (direct measurement)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If risk repricing is the driver, next-period outflows should remain elevated (>$500M) even if they don't exceed $1.75B. If this was idiosyncratic rebalancing, outflows should drop sharply to near-zero next period.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The convergence of three simultaneous deteriorations (outflows, par deviation, ASE decline) is inconsistent with idiosyncratic rebalancing by a few depositors. The proportional withdrawal pattern (stable concentration) specifically rules out a Facility-specific trigger and points to system-wide confidence reassessment. One assumption: that the primary driver is Atlas-specific rather than external. I count this as one assumption because the available data is internally consistent with this explanation.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Consortium Has Not Deployed Despite Elevated Stress
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The Consortium has pre-defined deployment triggers set at CRITICAL (not ELEVATED), and their monitoring posture is simply adherence to protocol — nothing strategic is happening.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: N/A — null hypothesis holds as sufficient.
evidence_citations: Consortium deployment_status listed as 'monitoring' (direct observation)
Consortium established 6 months ago — insufficient operating history to know their deployment triggers (thesis context)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If the Consortium deploys at CRITICAL but not ELEVATED, this confirms the null (protocol adherence). If the Consortium does NOT deploy even at CRITICAL, the discipline interpretation fails and the inability interpretation gains weight.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: Per LZ-RC-004, the simplest sufficient explanation must be tested first. The Consortium was established to act as a backstop. It would be standard practice for such an entity to have defined intervention thresholds, and those thresholds may simply be higher than ELEVATED. Without knowing their mandate or deployment criteria, attributing strategic meaning to their inaction requires assumptions I don't need to make. The null is sufficient. Per LZ-EH-005, Layer 2 correctly identified this as genuinely ambiguous — the null hypothesis is one valid interpretation. However, I note that the null hypothesis's continued viability depends on the Consortium deploying when conditions worsen. If they fail to deploy at CRITICAL, the null must be revisited.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Yield Gap Massively Exceeds WARNING Threshold
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The yield gap has existed since the Facility's inception — this is not a new development but a known feature of the architecture. The Foundation always intended to subsidize yield as a growth strategy, and the current gap does not represent deterioration.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Even if the yield gap was always a deliberate growth subsidy, the sustainability of that subsidy is now in question because the system is simultaneously experiencing its worst outflow period. A subsidy that was sustainable during growth (new deposits partially funding existing yields) becomes unsustainable during contraction (outflows create net cash demand while the subsidy continues). The yield gap is not new, but its context has fundamentally changed: the Foundation is now paying above-market yields to a SHRINKING depositor base during a period of rising system stress. This changes the yield gap from a growth investment to a defensive expenditure — and defensive expenditures have shorter runways.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The Foundation has a very large capital base (unknown) and the current burn rate is immaterial relative to reserves. The yield gap is sustainable for years regardless of outflows.
assumptions: The Foundation's capital base is finite and the ~$2.87B annual subsidy rate is material relative to that base
The shift from growth-phase to contraction-phase changes the sustainability calculus
evidence_citations: 24.2% posted yield vs 7.4% estimated organic yield = 16.8pp gap (direct measurement, with caveat that organic yield is estimated)
$1.75B net outflows indicating contraction, not growth (direct measurement)
$17.1B deposits × 16.8pp gap = ~$2.87B implied annual subsidy (arithmetic from verified inputs)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Observable yield reduction would confirm subsidy pressure is binding. Alternatively, continued unchanged yield for 30+ days during sustained outflows would suggest the Foundation has sufficient runway (though would not prove indefinite sustainability).
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis fails because context matters: a growth subsidy during expansion and a defensive subsidy during contraction are economically different even if the nominal gap is unchanged. However, I flag this at 2 assumptions because the Foundation's actual capital base is unknown — it's possible the subsidy is immaterial to them. Per CL-002, I note the yield is unchanged and do not infer imminent cuts. The evidence says: the gap exists, it's large, and the context has shifted from growth to contraction. What happens next depends on Foundation runway, which I do not know.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: ASE Price Decline Approaching Elevated Threshold
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The 11.8% ASE decline is driven by broader market conditions or normal volatility for a relatively young equity instrument, not Atlas-specific systemic stress.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: The ASE decline is partially mechanically driven by the swap mechanism: $650M in AFN redemptions produced 6.5M new ASE, adding 1.4% to supply. Redemption recipients who are exiting the Atlas ecosystem would sell this ASE immediately, creating direct selling pressure. The 11.8% decline during a period of verifiable supply increase (1.4%) and verified system stress (outflows, par deviation) is more parsimoniously explained by Atlas-specific factors than by coincidental broader market correlation.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Broader risk-off sentiment in the asset class is driving both ASE and other similar instruments down simultaneously.
assumptions: ASE price is primarily driven by Atlas-specific factors rather than broader market conditions in this period

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Reserve Coverage Ratio Below WARNING Level
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The coverage ratio has been trending down gradually and the current 17.8% represents the continuation of a known trajectory, not a new risk signal.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Even if the trajectory was known, the current level (17.8%) combined with mark-to-market losses on collateral and no new acquisitions for 12 days represents a qualitative change. The Consortium's last acquisition was Day -12, meaning they stopped building reserves before the current stress episode. Combined with outflows approaching critical, the coverage ratio is now declining through the denominator effect (outstanding AFN supply is not declining as fast as reserves are losing value). The absence of defensive action (no new acquisitions) during the worst stress period observed is significant per LZ-EH-003.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: The Consortium deliberately paused acquisitions to conserve cash for potential direct market intervention, and the declining ratio is a planned consequence of reserve prioritization.
assumptions: The absence of new acquisitions for 12 days during elevated stress reflects a constraint or strategic choice that is itself concerning
evidence_citations: 17.8% coverage ratio below 20% WARNING (direct measurement)
No new acquisitions since Day -12 (direct observation)
Mark-to-market losses on existing collateral — qualitative confirmation (direct observation)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: New collateral acquisition would signal the Consortium is actively managing the ratio. Continued inaction combined with further ratio decline toward 15% TRIGGERED would confirm that the coverage trajectory is unmanaged.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis is insufficient because 17.8% is not just a point on a trajectory — it's a point below a defined WARNING threshold during unprecedented stress, with no visible counter-action. The ratio's proximity to TRIGGERED (15%, only 2.8pp away) during a period of active collateral value deterioration (mark-to-market losses) means the effective ratio may already be closer to TRIGGERED than the headline 17.8% suggests.

Per LZ-EPH-002, I cannot attach high confidence to an unsustainability conclusion without the key variable. The null hypothesis — that we simply don't know — is honest and correct. The directional concern is valid (large subsidy during contraction), but the temporal claim (unsustainable) is unverifiable.

thesis_confirmed.key_evidence: The swap mechanism IS functioning (1.4% supply growth, within bounds). The system IS processing redemptions without technical failure. If outflows stabilize, ASE finds support, and the Consortium deploys successfully at CRITICAL, the thesis that 'the architecture holds under stress' would be confirmed. However, the current trajectory is against this outcome — confirmed requires reversal of multiple deteriorating trends simultaneously.
thesis_confirmed.timeline: 2-4 weeks — if outflows stabilize in next 1-2 periods and par deviation narrows below $0.50, the thesis survives this stress test. Each period of continued deterioration reduces this probability.

thesis_modified.modification: The architecture holds mechanically but requires permanent yield reduction and a smaller Facility footprint. The thesis modifies from 'self-sustaining architecture at scale' to 'viable architecture at reduced scale with lower yields.' This implies AFN continues to function but with a smaller, more concentrated institutional base and yields closer to organic (7-12% range). The system survives but the investment thesis changes significantly — lower yields mean less institutional demand, smaller deposits, and potentially lower ASE valuation.
thesis_modified.key_evidence: The swap mechanism works at current moderate volumes. Outflows are reducing the subsidy burden (natural brake). If the system reaches a new equilibrium at $12-14B deposits with 12-15% yield, the architecture may stabilize. Key assumption: Foundation can manage the transition without triggering panic.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: Two kill switches at WARNING (#2 and #3), compound stress at ELEVATED, outflows $250M from CRITICAL, ASE 3.2pp from third compound leg. The system is one moderate shock from CRITICAL transition. If outflows breach $2B, the reflexive feedback loop (outflows → redemptions → ASE minting → ASE decline → worse swap ratio → more outflows) could become self-reinforcing. Consortium inaction means no active counter-pressure. Foundation runway unknown but subsidy is large.
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Next-period outflows exceeding $2B AND AFN deviation widening past $1.00 WARNING AND ASE declining past 15% elevated threshold. If all three compound legs reach elevated simultaneously AND the Consortium still does not deploy, the architecture is likely failing. The most definitive falsification signal: swap mechanism ASE supply growth exceeding 5% per period, indicating the mechanism is creating more dilution than the system can absorb.

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Currently orderly but with rapidly diminishing buffer to disorderly. The system is processing redemptions, the swap mechanism functions, and no technical failures have occurred. However, the margin between current conditions and disorderly stress is dangerously thin: $250M additional outflows would breach the CRITICAL outflow threshold, 3.2pp additional ASE decline would activate the third compound leg, and the Consortium has not deployed, meaning there is no active counter-pressure. The system is orderly because nothing has broken yet, not because stabilizing forces are actively managing the stress.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: The most proximate catalyst is a single large depositor withdrawal of >$250M, which would breach the $2B CRITICAL outflow threshold. Given ~298 depositors averaging ~$57M, multiple depositors have individual positions large enough to trigger this alone. Secondary catalyst: a negative news event or analyst downgrade that shifts the 'temporary volatility' narrative, potentially triggering coordinated withdrawal behavior. Tertiary catalyst: further mark-to-market losses on Consortium collateral that push effective coverage below 15% TRIGGERED. The system does not need a large exogenous shock — it needs only a moderate continuation of current trends for 1-2 more periods.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: Two of three legs are pre-loaded at ELEVATED (AFN deviation $0.58 > $0.50, Facility outflows $1.75B > $500M). The third leg (ASE decline 11.8%) is 3.2pp from joining them at ELEVATED (15%). Pre-loading means: (1) The force required to reach CRITICAL on any individual leg is reduced because the other legs are already contributing stress. (2) The reflexive feedback loop between legs means deterioration in one amplifies the others. (3) The Consortium's decision not to deploy means there is no active force pushing any leg back from ELEVATED. The system is pre-loaded with no counter-pressure active. Per the thesis: 'the break point of a pre-loaded structure is LOWER than the break point of an unloaded structure.' This is now empirically relevant rather than theoretical.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The thesis posits that the debt-to-equity swap mechanism can maintain AFN par value under stress. The current stress test IS the mechanism being tested — and it is simultaneously the highest risk to the thesis AND the highest potential validation of the thesis. If the system processes $1.75B+ in outflows, $650M in swap redemptions, and 11.8% ASE decline WITHOUT breaking the peg beyond $1.00 WARNING, that is significant evidence that the architecture works under stress. But if the same stress pushes the system past CRITICAL and into a reflexive spiral, the architecture has failed its purpose. The thesis cannot be validated without being tested, and the test itself carries existential risk. Layer 4 must grapple with this directly: the current conditions are simultaneously the best and worst case for the thesis. The mechanism is being stress-tested in real-time, and the outcome is not yet determined. The honest assessment is that we are watching the thesis's defining moment unfold — and the next 1-2 periods will likely be decisive.

_layer3_raw.inference_summary: Player behavior reveals a system in a classic game-theoretic trap: all three operator-side players (Foundation, Consortium, Facility) are choosing inaction — maintaining yield, withholding reserves, not deploying — while depositors are actively withdrawing. This creates an asymmetry where defensive forces are static while offensive forces (outflows, price decline) are dynamic. The most critical finding is not any individual metric but the PATTERN of simultaneous deterioration across mechanically linked instruments with no active counter-pressure. Layer 4 must focus on three decisions: (1) whether the Consortium's non-deployment at ELEVATED is discipline or inability — this determines whether a backstop exists, (2) whether the next period's outflows breach the $250M gap to CRITICAL — this determines whether the reflexive feedback loop activates, and (3) whether the thesis is being validated or falsified by the current stress test — because both are simultaneously possible and the answer depends entirely on what happens in the next 1-2 periods.

knowledge_audit.gap_identified: Critical gap: I do not know the Foundation's total capital base, runway, or funding sources. Without knowing how much capital backs the subsidy, I cannot assess how long it can be sustained. The $2.87B annual burn rate is meaningless without a denominator. Additionally, the burn rate assumes static deposits — if deposits decline (which they are), the absolute subsidy requirement also declines.
knowledge_audit.post_audit_assessment: Score 7. The arithmetic is sound but the sustainability assessment requires Foundation capital data I don't have. Per LZ-EPH-002, I cannot attach high confidence to an unsustainability conclusion without knowing the runway. knowledge_audit.audit_impact: Reduced from 8 to 7 due to missing Foundation capital data. Per LZ-EPH-001, I declare Foundation runway as unknown rather than inferring it.

compound_stress.pre_loaded_detail: Two of three legs are at ELEVATED (AFN deviation at $0.58 > $0.50, Facility outflows at $1.75B > $500M). The third leg (ASE decline at 11.8%) is approaching the 15% elevated threshold with deteriorating trajectory. Pre-loading means the force required to reach CRITICAL is reduced. Specifically: Facility outflows need only an additional $250M to hit the $2B critical threshold (achievable from a single large depositor withdrawal). ASE needs only an additional 3.2pp decline to breach the elevated threshold. The system is one moderate shock away from either three-leg ELEVATED or transition toward CRITICAL conditions.
compound_stress.stress_chain_proximity: HIGH. The system is at ELEVATED with deteriorating trajectory on all three legs. The path to CRITICAL requires: (a) $250M additional outflows to breach $2B (Facility leg), OR (b) 3.2pp additional ASE decline to make all three legs elevated simultaneously. The reflexive feedback mechanism (outflows → redemptions → ASE minting → ASE price pressure → worse swap ratio → more minting) creates a potential self-reinforcing cycle that has not yet been tested. Consortium non-deployment at ELEVATED means there is no active counter-pressure. The Compound Stress Matrix break point is empirically untested at this level.
compound_stress.note: The thesis Compound Stress Matrix references USD/JPY, JGB 10Y, and Brent Crude as the three legs in the original prompt template, but the actual thesis context defines the three legs as AFN deviation, Facility outflows, and ASE price decline. I have reported on the actual thesis-defined legs. USD/JPY, JGB 10Y, and Brent Crude data are not present in the current market data and are not part of this thesis's Compound Stress Matrix.

(5) ASE declining 11.8% approaching the third compound stress leg at 15% — trajectory matters as much as level (+2). Partially offsetting: swap mechanism operational within bounds (-1), analyst consensus not yet shifted to bearish (-1). Net: +12 from previous 62 = 74. Per LZ-EPH-002, bear pressure assessment is proportional to the evidence: multiple verified deteriorations across direct kill switches with significant but quantifiable uncertainties remaining.
